The findings could reveal TAP/NXF1-HBc interaction as a novel therapeutic target for virus-associated cancers.
proposals.novelty: While TAP/NXF1's role in general RNA export is well-known, its specific hijacking by a viral protein to alter the export of a pro-metastatic RNA repertoire in lung cells is unexplored. This connects Chang's expertise in RNA-binding proteins and metastasis to viral oncology in a non-canonical HBV target organ.

proposals.title: Phosphorylation-dependent regulation of nuclear import receptor binding by the Hepatitis B virus core protein ARD: a novel phosphoswitch mechanism in viral oncogenesis
proposals.motivation: The Hepatitis B virus (HBV) core protein (HBc) is a key driver of viral replication and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) progression. Its nucleocytoplasmic shuttling, regulated by phosphorylation of its arginine-rich domain (ARD), is critical for viral lifecycle steps like genome packaging and delivery, but the precise mechanistic phosphocode controlling its interaction with nuclear transport receptors remains undefined. Elucidating this code is essential for understanding HBV pathogenesis and identifying novel therapeutic targets for HBV-associated HCC.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific phosphorylation patterns on the HBc ARD act as molecular switches, directly modulating the binding affinity of importin-α/β and CRM1 to expose or occlude the embedded nuclear localization signal (NLS) and nuclear export signal (NES), thereby dictating HBc subcellular localization.
proposals.method: We will generate a comprehensive panel of phosphomimetic (Ser/Thr to Asp/Glu) and phospho-deficient (Ser/Thr to Ala) variants of the HBc ARD. Using purified proteins, we will perform quantitative in vitro binding assays (e.g., surface plasmon resonance, co-immunoprecipitation) with recombinant importin-α/β and CRM1. These results will be validated in vivo by transfecting phospho-variants into HepG2 hepatoma cells and quantifying nuclear/cytoplasmic distribution via immunofluorescence and subcellular fractionation coupled with western blotting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic map linking specific ARD phosphorylation sites to the binding of nuclear transport machinery, defining the phosphoregulatory logic of HBc trafficking. It will establish a foundational model of viral phosphoswitches relevant to oncogenic viruses and may reveal novel, druggable interfaces to disrupt the HBV lifecycle and associated carcinogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While phospho-regulation of NLS/NES is established for cellular proteins like NFAT, its systematic application to decode the complex phosphorylation landscape of a viral oncoprotein like HBc is novel. This approach directly tests the functional consequence of each phosphosite on receptor binding, moving beyond correlative localization studies.

proposals.title: Structural mapping of the EGF-induced EGFR-STAT5 nuclear complex driving lung cancer progression
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that nuclear EGFR acts as a transcriptional co-activator, and its interaction with STAT5 is critical for promoting oncogenic gene expression and metastasis in cancers like lung adenocarcinoma. However, the precise structural interface between EGFR and STAT5 that forms upon EGF stimulation remains undefined, limiting the development of targeted inhibitors to disrupt this specific, pro-tumorigenic signaling axis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EGF stimulation induces a direct, phosphorylation-dependent interaction between the tyrosine kinase domain of EGFR and the SH2 domain of STAT5, and that this specific interface is essential for their nuclear translocation and subsequent oncogenic transcriptional activity.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of co-immunoprecipitation and domain-mapping using a series of EGFR (e.g., kinase-dead, deletion mutants) and STAT5 (SH2, coiled-coil, transactivation domain mutants) constructs in lung cancer cell lines. The specific interaction domains will be validated using in situ proximity ligation assay (PLA) to visualize nuclear complex formation. Computational modeling (using pre-2026 tools like HADDOCK 2.4) will integrate our mutagenesis data with known crystal structures of EGFR and STAT5 to generate a 3D model of the complex.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed structural map of the EGFR-STAT5 nuclear complex, identifying the critical protein domains required for its formation. It will establish a mechanistic link between EGF signaling, nuclear translocation, and STAT5-mediated transcription in lung cancer, offering a novel therapeutic target to inhibit metastasis without affecting global EGFR kinase activity.
proposals.novelty: While EGFR-STAT5 functional cooperation is known, the exact structural determinants of their direct physical interaction in the nucleus are unexplored. This proposal moves beyond correlative studies to define the actionable protein-protein interface, leveraging Dr. Chang's expertise in metastasis and expression regulation.

proposals.title: Live-Cell Imaging of EGF-Induced Nuclear Import Dynamics Reveals a Coordinated EGFR/STAT5 Transport Axis Promoting Lung Cancer Metastasis
proposals.motivation: Epidermal Growth Factor (EGF) signaling drives cancer progression and metastasis, in part through the nuclear translocation of its receptor (EGFR) and downstream effectors like STAT5, which regulate pro-metastatic gene expression (e.g., fibronectin, CD44). Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Chang's work, have linked these nuclear events to metastasis but have relied on endpoint assays, missing the real-time kinetics and coordination that may dictate transcriptional outcomes and therapeutic vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EGF stimulation induces rapid, coordinated nuclear import of EGFR and STAT5 in lung cancer cells, and that the kinetics and synchrony of this transport directly regulate the expression of metastasis-associated genes like CD44 and fibronectin.
proposals.method: We will use live-cell fluorescence microscopy in lung adenocarcinoma cell lines (e.g., A549, H1975) expressing endogenously tagged EGFR-mNeonGreen and STAT5-mScarlet via CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing. Cells will be serum-starved and imaged on a spinning-disk confocal system before and after EGF stimulation. Nuclear transport kinetics (import rates, time to peak nuclear fluorescence) will be quantified using automated segmentation in CellProfiler and tracked with the TrackMate plugin in Fiji/ImageJ. Pharmacological inhibition (e.g., HDAC inhibitors, importin blockers) and RNAi against specific nuclear pore components will test transport mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, real-time map of coordinated EGFR/STAT5 nuclear import in lung cancer, linking specific transport kinetics to metastatic gene expression profiles. It will identify nuclear transport as a dynamic, rate-limiting step in EGF-driven metastasis, offering a novel conceptual framework and potential therapeutic targets (e.g., specific nuclear import pathways) to disrupt pro-metastatic signaling.
proposals.novelty: While nuclear EGFR and STAT5 are known, their real-time co-transport dynamics have never been visualized; this approach moves beyond static snapshots to reveal the temporal regulation of this critical oncogenic signaling module, integrating Dr. Chang's expertise in metastasis-associated gene regulation with advanced live-cell imaging.

proposals.title: Genome-wide CRISPR screening identifies novel regulators of the nuclear EGFR-STAT5-Aurora-A axis in lung adenocarcinoma metastasis
proposals.motivation: Nuclear EGFR (nEGFR) drives oncogenic transcription and is linked to poor prognosis in lung cancer. Its partnership with STAT5 to upregulate Aurora-A, a key mitotic kinase, promotes metastasis, but the full complement of genes required for this signaling cascade remains unknown. Identifying these components is critical for developing targeted therapies to disrupt this pro-metastatic pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genome-wide CRISPR knockout screen will reveal novel and essential genes, beyond canonical EGFR/STAT5 signaling, that are required for nEGFR-STAT5-mediated Aurora-A expression and its subsequent functional outputs in lung cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen in a metastatic lung adenocarcinoma cell line (e.g., H1975) with an established nEGFR-STAT5-Aurora-A axis. Cells transduced with the Brunello sgRNA library will be selected based on loss of a GFP reporter driven by the Aurora-A promoter or sorted for low Aurora-A protein expression via FACS. sgRNA enrichment/depletion will be analyzed using MAGeCK (v0.5.9.4) to identify statistically significant hits. Candidate genes will be validated via individual sgRNA knockout and assessed for effects on Aurora-A mRNA/protein levels, STAT5 phosphorylation, and in vitro metastatic phenotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of genetic dependencies for the nEGFR-STAT5-Aurora-A pathway, revealing new therapeutic targets. It will mechanistically expand our understanding of how nuclear EGFR coordinates transcriptional complexes, directly aligning with Dr. Chang's expertise in transcription factor-mediated metastasis (e.g., Sp1/miR-3194-5p/CD44) and pathway disruption therapies.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR screens for EGFR signaling exist, none have specifically interrogated the nuclear EGFR-STAT5 transcriptional module regulating Aurora-A. This approach moves beyond characterizing known interactors to discover novel, context-specific regulators of this critical metastatic axis.

proposals.title: Targeted Disruption of Nuclear EGFR-STAT5 Signaling Attenuates Metastatic Progression in Lung Adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Nuclear EGFR (nEGFR) acts as a transcriptional co-activator, and its interaction with STAT5 is implicated in driving oncogenic gene expression programs in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), contributing to therapy resistance and metastasis. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Chang's work on transcription factors like Sp1 and CD44 in lung cancer, highlight the importance of such nuclear complexes, but the specific consequences of inhibiting the nEGFR-STAT5 protein-protein interaction remain unexplored as a therapeutic strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific pharmacological inhibition of the nEGFR-STAT5 interaction will suppress a pro-metastatic gene expression signature, leading to reduced invasion, stemness, and metastasis in lung adenocarcinoma models.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-guided peptidomimetic inhibitor (based on pre-2026 crystal structures of EGFR/STAT5 interfaces) to disrupt the nEGFR-STAT5 complex in NSCLC cell lines (e.g., A549, H1975). Effects on global gene expression will be assessed via RNA-seq, with pathway analysis using GSEA and Ingenuity Pathway Analysis. Functional consequences will be measured by transwell invasion assays, tumorsphere formation, and in vivo metastasis using a tail-vein injection mouse model. Validation of target engagement will utilize co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific transcriptomic and phenotypic outcomes of nEGFR-STAT5 disruption, identifying a novel vulnerability in lung cancer. It will provide pre-clinical proof-of-concept for a targeted protein-protein interaction inhibitor in NSCLC metastasis, potentially offering a strategy to circumvent resistance to conventional EGFR kinase inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While the role of nEGFR is known, this proposal moves beyond correlative studies to directly test the functional necessity of its interaction with STAT5 using a targeted inhibitor, a mechanistic approach distinct from broad HDAC or kinase inhibition used in prior studies.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Cell Death Specificity of ALZ003: A Comprehensive Assessment of Apoptotic and Ferroptotic Pathways in Lung Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death implicated in tumor suppression and therapy response. However, many pharmacological inducers can trigger multiple, parallel death pathways, confounding mechanistic understanding and therapeutic optimization. Determining whether the experimental compound ALZ003 exclusively induces ferroptosis is critical for its rational development as a targeted anti-cancer agent, particularly in lung cancer where metastasis remains a major clinical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ALZ003 induces cell death in lung cancer cells solely through the ferroptosis pathway, without concurrent activation of classical apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will treat a panel of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell lines with ALZ003 and perform multi-modal assessment. Apoptosis will be quantified via flow cytometry for Annexin V/PI staining, caspase-3/7 activity assays, and immunoblotting for cleaved PARP. Ferroptosis will be confirmed by rescue with ferrostatin-1 and liproxstatin-1, measurement of lipid peroxidation (C11-BODIPY 581/591 flow cytometry), and depletion of glutathione. Specificity will be further tested using genetic (shRNA knockdown of ACSL4) and pharmacological (Z-VAD-FMK pan-caspase inhibitor) blockade of each pathway. RNA-seq will profile expression changes in death-related genes.
proposals.contribution: This study will definitively characterize the mechanism of action of ALZ003, clarifying its potential as a selective ferroptosis inducer. It will contribute a rigorous methodological framework for disentangling overlapping cell death pathways in cancer biology. The findings could inform the development of ALZ003 or its analogs as targeted therapies, especially for tumors resistant to apoptosis.
proposals.novelty: While ferroptosis inducers are known, systematic validation to exclude co-occurring apoptosis in the context of a novel compound like ALZ003 is not routine. This work integrates the researcher's expertise in lung cancer progression and metastasis with a precise, multi-parametric analysis of cell death, moving beyond correlative observations to establish causal mechanism.

proposals.title: Identification of Context-Dependent E3 Ubiquitin Ligases for the Androgen Receptor in Glioblastoma Progression
proposals.motivation: The androgen receptor (AR) is a key driver in multiple cancers, but its role and regulation in glioblastoma (GBM) remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 evidence suggests AR signaling influences GBM cell proliferation and survival, yet the post-translational mechanisms controlling AR protein stability in the brain tumor microenvironment are unknown. Identifying the E3 ligases that ubiquitinate AR in GBM could reveal novel therapeutic targets for this aggressive malignancy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, contrary to the established role of FBXL2 in other contexts, specific and alternative E3 ubiquitin ligases target AR for degradation in glioblastoma cells, and their identity is modulated by tumor microenvironmental cues such as hypoxia or growth factor signaling.
proposals.method: We will perform a focused siRNA screen targeting a pre-defined library of ~400 human E3 ligases in AR-positive GBM cell lines (e.g., U87, T98G) under normoxic, hypoxic, and EGF-stimulated conditions, monitoring AR protein levels via high-throughput immunofluorescence. Candidate ligases will be validated through co-immunoprecipitation and in vitro ubiquitination assays. The functional impact of candidate E3s on AR-driven phenotypes will be assessed using assays for cell viability, invasion, and stemness, aligning with Dr. Chang's expertise in metastasis and progression.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the post-translational regulatory landscape of AR in GBM, potentially identifying novel E3 ligase-AR axes. It will provide mechanistic insight into how the GBM microenvironment influences AR stability and signaling. The findings could establish new targets for disrupting AR signaling in GBM, expanding the therapeutic paradigm beyond classical androgen-dependent cancers.
proposals.novelty: While FBXL2 is recognized as an AR E3 ligase in prostate cancer, a systematic search for context-specific AR ligases in the distinct molecular landscape of glioblastoma has not been conducted. This proposal directly challenges the notion of a single, universal AR E3 ligase by interrogating its regulation under GBM-relevant stresses.

proposals.title: Targeting Androgen Receptor-Mediated Therapy Resistance in Lung Adenocarcinoma with ALZ003: Efficacy and Mechanistic Insights
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that the androgen receptor (AR) is expressed and functionally active in non-prostate cancers, including lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD), where it promotes tumor progression, metastasis, and resistance to standard therapies. Given Dr. Chang's expertise in lung cancer progression and metastasis, and the recent development of AR-targeting agents like ALZ003, there is a critical need to evaluate its efficacy in AR-positive LUAD models where AR drives a resistant phenotype.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ALZ003 will inhibit AR signaling in AR-positive lung adenocarcinoma cells, leading to reduced expression of AR-driven pro-metastatic genes (such as CD44 and fibronectin), and will overcome therapy resistance in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.method: We will utilize a panel of AR-positive LUAD cell lines (e.g., A549, H1792) with established resistance to EGFR-TKIs or chemotherapy. Cell viability, colony formation, and invasion assays will be performed with ALZ003 treatment. Mechanistically, we will assess AR nuclear translocation (immunofluorescence), AR-target gene expression (qRT-PCR for CD44, FN1), and downstream signaling (Western blot). In vivo, we will employ orthotopic lung metastasis and patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models to evaluate ALZ003's effect on tumor growth and metastasis, with endpoints including bioluminescent imaging and immunohistochemical analysis of AR, CD44, and Ki67.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the therapeutic potential of ALZ003 beyond prostate cancer, specifically in AR-positive LUAD. It will provide a mechanistic link between AR inhibition and the suppression of key metastasis-associated pathways previously studied by the PI (e.g., CD44, fibronectin), offering a novel combinatorial strategy to overcome therapy resistance.
proposals.novelty: While AR's role in prostate cancer is well-known, its targeting in lung cancer with next-generation inhibitors like ALZ003 is underexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Chang's focus on metastasis-regulating proteins (CD44, fibronectin) with a direct test of a clinical-stage AR inhibitor in lung cancer models of therapy resistance.

proposals.title: Identification of Novel FBXL2 Substrates Regulating Ferroptosis Sensitivity and Temozolomide Resistance in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) remains a lethal brain tumor with poor prognosis, largely due to intrinsic resistance to therapies like temozolomide (TMZ). Pre-2026 research established FBXL2 as an E3 ubiquitin ligase targeting proteins for degradation, with specific links to cancer progression and metastasis. Recent findings suggest FBXL2 substrates may regulate ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death, and contribute to TMZ resistance, but the full repertoire of FBXL2 targets in GBM is unknown. Identifying these substrates is critical for understanding therapeutic resistance mechanisms and developing new combination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FBXL2 ubiquitinates and degrades key proteins that suppress ferroptosis or promote DNA repair, and that identification of these novel substrates will reveal actionable targets to overcome TMZ resistance in GBM.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged proteomics approach in patient-derived GBM stem-like cells (GSCs). First, we will perform affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS) using FLAG-tagged FBXL2 to identify interacting proteins. Second, we will utilize ubiquitin remnant profiling (diGly proteomics) upon FBXL2 knockdown or overexpression to pinpoint proteins with altered ubiquitination. Candidate substrates will be validated through cycloheximide chase assays and in vitro ubiquitination. Functional screening via siRNA against candidates will assess their role in TMZ sensitivity and ferroptosis (measured by lipid peroxidation and cell death assays). Bioinformatics analysis will integrate our data with public GBM expression and survival datasets (e.g., TCGA) to prioritize clinically relevant targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of the FBXL2 ubiquitin ligase network in GBM, directly linking specific substrates to the ferroptosis pathway and chemoresistance. It will provide novel mechanistic insights into how post-translational regulation governs therapeutic response, identifying potential biomarkers for patient stratification and new drug targets, such as the identified substrates or the FBXL2-substrate interaction interface, for combination therapy with TMZ.
proposals.novelty: While FBXL2's role in other cancers is emerging, its substrate landscape in GBM is entirely unexplored. This proposal uniquely connects FBXL2-mediated ubiquitination to two critical, converging resistance mechanisms in GBM—ferroptosis evasion and TMZ resistance—a link not established in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Synergistic induction of ferroptosis by ALZ003 and erastin overcomes temozolomide resistance in glioblastoma by targeting CD44 and GPX4
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) remains incurable, with temozolomide (TMZ) resistance being a major therapeutic failure. Pre-2026 research has established ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death, as a promising vulnerability in therapy-resistant cancers, including GBM. However, single-agent ferroptosis inducers often lack potency or face compensatory resistance mechanisms, necessitating rational combination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the novel compound ALZ003, which disrupts pentraxin 3/CD44 interaction, will synergize with the canonical ferroptosis inducer erastin to overcome TMZ resistance in GBM by concurrently inhibiting CD44-mediated survival signaling and glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) activity, leading to lethal lipid peroxidation.
proposals.method: We will establish patient-derived TMZ-resistant GBM cell lines and orthotopic mouse models. Synergy will be quantified using the Chou-Talalay combination index method on cell viability assays. Mechanistically, we will assess lipid peroxidation (C11-BODIPY flow cytometry), intracellular iron and glutathione levels, and expression of ferroptosis regulators (GPX4, SLC7A11, ACSL4) via western blot and qPCR. The role of CD44 will be validated using shRNA knockdown. In vivo efficacy and survival benefit will be evaluated in orthotopic models, with tumors analyzed for ferroptosis markers (4-HNE, PTGS2) and metastasis.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, synergistic drug combination for TMZ-resistant GBM, directly linking the CD44 pathway to ferroptosis susceptibility. It will provide a mechanistic rationale for combining pathway-targeted agents like ALZ003 with direct ferroptosis inducers, offering a translatable therapeutic strategy beyond single-agent approaches.
proposals.novelty: While ferroptosis inducers are being explored in GBM, combining them with a CD44-pathway disruptor like ALZ003 is novel. This approach uniquely targets a metastasis-linked surface protein (CD44) to potentiate ferroptosis, bridging insights from Chang's prior work on CD44 in lung and breast cancer with the emerging field of ferroptosis therapy.

proposals.title: Microdose ALZ003 induces adaptive AR signaling and pro-metastatic phenotypic switching in lung adenocarcinoma cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that sub-therapeutic drug exposure can paradoxically promote cancer cell survival and metastasis through adaptive signaling. The compound ALZ003, a known modulator of protein-protein interactions, has been studied at cytotoxic doses, but its effects at very low, non-cytotoxic concentrations are unknown. Given Dr. Chang's focus on lung cancer progression and metastasis, understanding how microdose ALZ003 influences androgen receptor (AR) signaling and cellular adaptation is critical, as AR signaling is implicated in lung cancer progression and therapy resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that microdose, non-cytotoxic ALZ003 treatment will induce adaptive AR signaling, leading to transcriptional reprogramming that enhances cellular plasticity and pro-metastatic traits in lung adenocarcinoma cells.
proposals.method: We will treat a panel of AR-positive lung adenocarcinoma cell lines (e.g., A549, H1792) with pico- to nanomolar concentrations of ALZ003, confirming non-cytotoxicity via MTT assays. AR signaling activation and downstream targets (e.g., PSA/KLK3) will be assessed by qRT-PCR, western blot, and an AR-responsive luciferase reporter assay. To evaluate phenotypic adaptation, we will perform RNA-seq to identify differentially expressed genes, with a focus on metastasis-associated pathways (e.g., EMT, integrin signaling). Functional validation will include transwell migration/invasion assays and 3D spheroid culture. Interaction networks will be analyzed using STRING and Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA).
proposals.contribution: This study will define a novel, non-cytotoxic mechanism by which ALZ003 modulates AR signaling and cellular adaptation in lung cancer. It will identify specific transcriptional programs and phenotypic changes driven by microdose exposure, contributing to the understanding of how low-dose drug environments may inadvertently fuel cancer progression. The findings could inform safer dosing strategies and reveal new adaptive signaling vulnerabilities.
proposals.novelty: While ALZ003's effects at therapeutic doses are documented, this proposal uniquely investigates its impact at microdose levels, linking non-cytotoxic AR modulation to adaptive, pro-metastatic cellular reprogramming—a paradigm distinct from its intended therapeutic action.

proposals.title: Preemptive targeting of the Androgen Receptor with ALZ003 to delay temozolomide resistance in lung adenocarcinoma
proposals.motivation: Temozolomide (TMZ) resistance remains a major clinical challenge in treating lung adenocarcinoma, often leading to disease progression and metastasis. Pre-2026 evidence indicates that the Androgen Receptor (AR) can drive tumor progression and therapy resistance in non-prostate cancers, including lung, but its role in the *emergence* of TMZ resistance is poorly understood. Applying targeted therapy early, before resistance is clinically evident, could significantly improve patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AR signaling is activated as an adaptive survival mechanism in TMZ-sensitive lung adenocarcinoma cells, and that preemptive co-treatment with the AR inhibitor ALZ003 will suppress this adaptive response, thereby delaying or preventing the acquisition of TMZ resistance.
proposals.method: We will use established TMZ-sensitive human lung adenocarcinoma cell lines (e.g., A549). Cells will be treated with TMZ alone, ALZ003 alone, or a combination, with longitudinal monitoring of viability and clonogenic survival. We will assess AR expression (protein and mRNA levels) and activity (via luciferase reporter assays) during treatment. To model the emergence of resistance, we will generate TMZ-tolerant persister cells and profile changes in AR and its downstream targets (e.g., CD44, fibronectin) using qPCR, western blot, and RNA-seq. Metastatic potential will be evaluated using in vitro invasion assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, preemptive therapeutic strategy for lung adenocarcinoma by targeting AR before resistance is established. It will elucidate the molecular mechanism by which AR activation contributes to TMZ tolerance, potentially linking it to known pro-metastatic pathways like CD44. The findings could shift the paradigm for TMZ use from monotherapy to rational combination regimens.
proposals.novelty: While AR inhibition is established in prostate cancer, its preemptive use to *forestall* chemotherapy resistance in lung cancer is unexplored. This approach moves beyond targeting fully resistant tumors to intercepting the adaptive processes that lead to resistance, aligning with the researcher's expertise in metastasis-related gene regulation (CD44, fibronectin) during progression.

proposals.title: Comparative Impact of Post-Translational Modifications on Metastatic Gene Expression: A Focus on Acetylation and Phosphorylation in Lung Cancer
proposals.motivation: The role of phosphorylation changes in cancer progression is well-studied, but its primacy over other modifications like acetylation, methylation, or ubiquitination in driving metastatic gene expression programs remains unclear. Given Dr. Chang's recent work on HDACs, Sp1, and CD44 in lung cancer, understanding the hierarchy of PTMs is critical for designing targeted therapies that disrupt key oncogenic signaling nodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that histone deacetylase (HDAC)-mediated acetylation changes, rather than phosphorylation state changes, are the primary molecular event driving the extinction of metastasis-suppressive gene programs, such as those involving CD44 and fibronectin, in non-small cell lung cancer.
proposals.method: We will use isogenic lung adenocarcinoma cell lines (e.g., A549) with inducible shRNAs against specific HDACs (e.g., HDAC1, SIRT1) and kinases/phosphatases (e.g., AKT, PTEN). Global PTM dynamics will be profiled using mass spectrometry-based phosphoproteomics and acetylomics. Causal relationships will be established by integrating this data with RNA-seq and ChIP-seq for Sp1 and RNA-binding proteins (e.g., as in HNSCC work) following EGF or estradiol stimulation, using multivariate regression and Bayesian network inference to model PTM hierarchy.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the relative contribution of acetylation versus phosphorylation in a defined metastatic signaling axis, moving beyond correlative studies. It will identify the dominant 'master' PTM for therapeutic targeting in lung cancer metastasis and provide a computational framework for prioritizing PTM crosstalk events.
proposals.novelty: While PTM crosstalk is recognized, systematically testing acetylation's primacy over phosphorylation in extinguishing specific metastatic gene programs, using Dr. Chang's established molecular models (Sp1/CD44, fibronectin), is a novel and direct experimental approach.

proposals.title: Delineating Cytotoxic from Pathway-Specific Effects: A Dose-Response and Transcriptomic Analysis of Ursolic Acid in Lung Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: Ursolic acid (UA) is a natural pentacyclic triterpenoid with reported anti-cancer properties, including inhibition of metastasis-related pathways like Sp1 and CD44. However, many studies use high, potentially cytotoxic concentrations, conflating specific therapeutic effects with general cell death. Clarifying this distinction is critical for developing UA as a targeted therapeutic, especially in lung cancer where pathways like CD44 and Sp1/miR-3194-5p drive progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at high concentrations, UA's anti-proliferative effects in lung cancer cells are primarily due to non-specific cytotoxicity, whereas at lower, sub-cytotoxic doses, its activity is mediated through specific inhibition of metastasis-associated pathways such as Sp1/CD44 signaling.
proposals.method: We will treat a panel of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell lines with a wide range of UA doses. Cell viability and apoptosis will be assessed via MTT and Annexin V/PI flow cytometry to define cytotoxic vs. sub-cytotoxic ranges. RNA-seq will be performed on cells treated with low (sub-cytotoxic) and high (cytotoxic) doses. Differential expression analysis (using DESeq2) and gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) will identify pathway-specific changes at low doses versus broad stress/death signatures at high doses. Key targets (e.g., SP1, CD44, fibronectin) will be validated by qPCR and western blot.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a clear, mechanistic framework for interpreting UA's anti-cancer activity, distinguishing its genuine molecular targets from secondary cytotoxic effects. It will offer crucial dosing guidelines for future pre-clinical studies of UA and similar natural compounds, enhancing their translational potential as specific metastasis inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While UA's effects are well-documented, this systematic dose-deconstruction integrating precise cytotoxicity assays with unbiased transcriptomics to segregate mechanism from toxicity is novel. It directly addresses a common but often overlooked confounder in natural product oncology research.

proposals.title: Mechanistic validation of ursolic acid anti-metastatic effects in lung cancer: comparative siRNA knockdown of COX-2 and VEGF targets
proposals.motivation: Ursolic acid (UA) is a promising natural compound with demonstrated anti-cancer properties, including inhibition of metastasis in lung cancer models. However, its precise molecular targets and mechanism of action remain incompletely defined, particularly regarding its effects on key pro-metastatic factors like COX-2 and VEGF. Validating these targets is crucial for developing more potent, targeted therapies against lung cancer progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anti-metastatic effects of UA treatment in lung cancer cells are mediated primarily through the specific downregulation of COX-2 and VEGF, and that siRNA-mediated knockdown of these targets will phenocopy the effects of UA treatment on invasion and migration.
proposals.method: We will use non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell lines (e.g., A549, H1299) with high metastatic potential. Cells will be transfected with validated siRNA pools targeting COX-2 (PTGS2) or VEGF-A, using a non-targeting siRNA as a control. Knockdown efficiency will be confirmed via qRT-PCR and western blotting. Functional assays for metastasis, including transwell invasion and wound-healing migration assays, will be performed on siRNA-treated cells and compared directly to parallel cultures treated with a defined concentration of UA. Gene expression changes in downstream pathways (e.g., EMT markers, CD44) will be analyzed by RNA-seq.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly validate COX-2 and VEGF as critical functional targets of UA in lung cancer metastasis, moving beyond correlative observations. It will provide a clearer mechanistic understanding of UA's action, strengthening the rationale for its therapeutic development. The findings may also identify a dominant target pathway, informing future combination or synthetic lethality strategies.
proposals.novelty: While UA's effects on COX-2/VEGF expression are known, a direct, head-to-head functional comparison between pharmacological inhibition (UA) and genetic knockdown of these specific targets in lung cancer metastasis models has not been systematically performed, offering a definitive validation strategy.

proposals.title: Induction of Ursolic Acid Resistance in Endometriotic Cells Reveals Compensatory HDAC and CD44 Pathways for Co-Targeted Therapy
proposals.motivation: Ursolic acid (UA) shows promising anti-proliferative and pro-apoptotic effects in various cancers, but its application in endometriosis is limited by potential resistance development. Understanding the compensatory pathways that emerge under UA pressure is critical for designing durable, multi-target therapies. This 'inverse problem' approach, systematically inducing resistance to identify targetable vulnerabilities, is under-explored in endometriosis despite its success in oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic, sub-lethal exposure of endometriotic cells to UA will select for resistant populations that upregulate specific survival pathways, such as HDAC activity and CD44-mediated signaling, which can be co-targeted to overcome resistance.
proposals.method: We will establish UA-resistant lines from primary human endometriotic stromal cells via long-term, dose-escalation culture. RNA-seq and proteomic profiling will identify differentially expressed genes and proteins in resistant vs. parental cells. Pathway enrichment analysis (using GSEA and Ingenuity Pathway Analysis) will pinpoint upregulated survival networks. Functional validation will involve siRNA knockdown or pharmacological inhibition (e.g., HDAC inhibitors like SAHA, anti-CD44 antibodies) in combination with UA, assessing cell viability, invasion (transwell assays), and markers of epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT).
proposals.contribution: This work will map the landscape of adaptive resistance to UA in endometriosis, identifying novel combinatorial targets to enhance therapeutic efficacy. It will provide a functional dataset linking specific resistance pathways (e.g., HDACs, CD44) to UA treatment, offering a preclinical rationale for targeted drug combinations. The study will establish a generalizable 'resistance induction' model for probing therapeutic vulnerabilities in endometriosis.
proposals.novelty: While resistance studies are common in cancer, applying this 'inverse' strategy to UA in endometriosis is novel. It uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in HDAC biology and CD44 interactions from lung and breast cancer into a new disease context to discover co-targetable pathways.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Angiogenic Factors Beyond VEGF in Bone Metastasis Healing: A Focus on Pentraxin 3 and CD44-Mediated Pathways
proposals.motivation: While VEGF-targeted therapies are a cornerstone of anti-angiogenic cancer treatment, their efficacy in promoting bone healing, particularly in the context of bone metastasis or post-resection defects, is limited and can cause adverse effects like abnormal vessel formation. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Chang's own work, highlights the critical role of alternative pathways, such as the pentraxin 3 (PTX3)/CD44 interaction, in regulating tumor progression and metastasis. Identifying a more effective pro-angiogenic target for bone repair could revolutionize regenerative strategies for cancer patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeting the PTX3/CD44 axis, or other specific factors like FGF-2 or PDGF-BB, will induce more robust, functional, and stable angiogenesis in bone healing models compared to VEGF-A alone, leading to superior osteointegration and repair.
proposals.method: We will utilize pre-2026 established in vitro and in vivo models. Human lung cancer cell lines (A549, H1299) and primary human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) will be treated with recombinant VEGF, FGF-2, PDGF-BB, or PTX3, and their CD44 levels will be modulated via siRNA (as in Chang's prior work). Angiogenic potential will be assessed via tube formation, migration, and proliferation assays. For in vivo validation, we will employ a murine calvarial defect model with localized, controlled delivery of each factor via hydrogel. Healing will be quantified longitudinally using micro-CT for bone volume and vessel perfusion, and histology for vessel maturity (α-SMA coverage) and bone formation.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly compare the bone regenerative angiogenic capacity of key factors, moving beyond the VEGF-centric paradigm. It will establish a ranked efficacy profile and could identify PTX3/CD44 modulation as a superior therapeutic target. The findings could directly inform the development of next-generation biomaterials and biologic therapies for bone defect healing, especially in oncological contexts.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely applies the mechanistic insight from cancer biology—specifically the PTX3/CD44 axis known to drive aggressive triple-negative breast cancer—to the challenge of bone regeneration, creating a direct translational bridge between Dr. Chang's expertise in metastasis and the unmet clinical need in bone healing.

ideas.title: Investigate valproate's protection against metabolic stress via modulation of ARD-dependent nuclear localization
ideas.description: This study investigates whether valproate's (VA) protective effect against insulin resistance in a combined high-fat diet and toxin model is mediated by its ability to modulate the nuclear import of transcriptional regulators. Specifically, it tests if VA treatment alters the expression, post-translational modification, or partnering of ARD-I or ARD-III proteins, enabling their nuclear localization in the stressed metabolic tissue despite their inherent lack of a strong intrinsic NLS. This approach directly links a drug's metabolic efficacy to a fundamental cellular transport mechanism, asking if VA can induce a conditional bypass of an absolute nuclear localization signal co-dependency. The outcome could reveal a new pharmacologic mechanism where metabolic protection is achieved by reprogramming the nucleo-cytoplasmic trafficking of key regulatory factors.

ideas.title: Adapting Nuclear Import Receptor Phosphoswitches to Map HBc ARD Phosphoregulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established concept of phosphorylation-dependent NLS/NES masking, used in fields like transcription factor regulation (e.g., NFAT), to solve the problem of how phosphorylation regulates HBc localization signals. The analogous solution involves defining phospho-switches that control receptor binding. Adapted to HBc, the research will systematically create phospho-variants of the ARD and employ in vitro nuclear transport binding assays (e.g., with importins and CRM1) coupled with in vivo localization studies in hepatoma cells. This approach will directly map the mechanistic impact of specific phosphorylation events on the functional exposure or occlusion of the HBc NLS and NES, defining the phosphoregulatory code for its subcellular trafficking.

ideas.title: Adapting SH3 Domain-Peptide Interaction Mapping to Define the HBc ARD-TAP/NXF1 Interface
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established methodology from mapping SH3 domain-peptide interactions to the virology problem of characterizing the HBc ARD interaction with TAP/NXF1. In cellular signaling, precise binding interfaces for modular domains are defined using peptide array scanning, mutagenesis, and structural validation. The adapted research idea treats TAP/NXF1 as the receptor domain and the HBc ARD as its ligand. It proposes using a comprehensive peptide array of the ARD with systematic alanine and arginine substitutions to biochemically map the precise binding site(s) for TAP/NXF1. Functional validation through co-immunoprecipitation and nuclear export assays will then directly link the identified critical ARD residues to the functional consequence of HBV capsid nuclear export.

ideas.title: Distinguishing Insulin Receptor Bypass from Residual Signaling: A Multi-Knockout and Pathway-Inhibition Framework for Vanillic Acid
ideas.description: This study is designed to rigorously test whether vanillic acid (VA) activates glucose uptake through a mechanism genuinely independent of insulin receptor (InsR) signaling. We employ L6 myotubes or 3T3-L1 adipocytes with dual genetic knockout of InsR and IGF-1R, eliminating major compensatory receptors. Glucose uptake and GLUT4 translocation are assayed under acute pharmacological inhibition of PI3K and Akt. The system is validated using the AMPK activator AICAR as a positive control for InsR-independent uptake. This design conclusively separates a novel VA pathway from artifacts of residual insulin-like signaling.

ideas.title: Targeting Non-Canonical Nuclear Export to Enhance VA's Metabolic Efficacy in Prediabetes
ideas.description: This study conducts a pilot clinical trial in prediabetic humans to test VA's effect on insulin resistance, while simultaneously analyzing patient-derived cells to determine if VA's efficacy correlates with inhibition of a specific, Leptomycin B-resistant nuclear export pathway. By challenging the assumption that all nuclear export signal (NES)-dependent cargo uses CRM1, we will identify the alternative export pathway for Hepatitis B core antigen (HBc) and test if VA's metabolic benefits are mediated through this same non-canonical route. This approach directly connects clinical outcomes with a fundamental discovery in cellular transport biology, potentially revealing a new class of metabolic drug targets within the nuclear pore complex.

ideas.title: Assess vanillic acid's chronic toxicity via its modulation of ARD nuclear import dependency
ideas.description: This study investigates the long-term safety profile of vanillic acid (VA) by integrating a chronic toxicity assessment with a molecular probe function. VA will be administered long-term in a model organism, and tissues will be analyzed not only for standard toxicity markers but also for the subcellular localization and functional state of ARD-I and ARD-III proteins. The core hypothesis is that chronic VA exposure, through its established antioxidant and metabolic effects, may create a cellular environment that could potentially modify or bypass the absolute NLS co-dependency of these ARDs. This approach tests if prolonged compound exposure can induce latent, weak NLS functionality, thereby linking a compound's toxicity profile directly to its ability to perturb a fundamental and tightly regulated nuclear import pathway.

ideas.title: Adapt Kinome Activity Mapping from Cancer Drug Discovery to Elucidate VA Signaling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cancer pharmacology, where kinome activity mapping is standard for deconvoluting kinase inhibitor signaling networks. The analogous approach uses multiplexed kinome activity assays (like peptide arrays) to identify primary kinase targets, which is then integrated with phosphoproteomic data to build causal network models. Adapted to vanillic acid (VA) research, we will first profile VA-induced changes in the activity of hundreds of human kinases using a commercial kinome array. This primary kinome data will then serve as a constraint to inform the analysis of high-throughput phosphoproteomics time-course data. The integrated analysis will computationally reconstruct the VA signaling network, distinguishing direct kinase modulation from downstream adaptive phosphorylation events, thereby providing a mechanistic, kinase-resolved map of VA action.

ideas.title: Using VA Dose-Duration as a Probe to Map the Non-CRM1 Export Pathway of HBc
ideas.description: This study fuses mechanistic discovery with therapeutic optimization by using dose-response and time-course studies of Valproic Acid (VA) as a dynamic probe to investigate the non-CRM1 export pathway of the Hepatitis B core protein (HBc). By systematically varying VA dosage and duration, we can correlate the degree of CRM1 inhibition (via Leptomycin B resistance) with changes in HBc subcellular localization and export kinetics, thereby mapping the sensitivity and capacity of the alternative pathway. This approach will identify not only the optimal VA regimen to maximally disrupt HBV replication by trapping HBc in the nucleus, but also reveal key kinetic parameters and potential druggable components of the non-canonical export machinery itself. The synergy lies in turning a treatment optimization study into a powerful method for pathway characterization, where therapeutic variables directly elucidate mechanistic biology.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Model Organism Portfolio' Approach from Cancer Research to T2D Model Development
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that the HFD-induced rodent model adequately reflects human T2D complexity for testing VA efficacy. Drawing an analogy from preclinical oncology, where over-reliance on simplistic xenograft models led to high clinical failure rates, we adapt their solution. Oncology addressed this by validating therapies across a portfolio of models (GEMMs, PDXs, organoids) to better mimic human disease heterogeneity. Adapted to T2D, the research idea is to establish and employ a standardized portfolio of rodent models—including polygenic, monogenic, and potentially microbiota-humanized models—as a prerequisite for VA efficacy studies. The core adapted idea is to test VA compounds not in a single model, but across this etiologically diverse panel, de-risking translation by requiring consistent therapeutic benefit across models that capture different facets of human T2D pathophysiology (genetic risk, severe metabolic disruption, environmental interaction) before proceeding to human trials.

ideas.title: Testing NES Function as a Context-Dependent Molecular Switch
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ARD-II and IV NESs function independently by proposing they are molecular switches whose activity is context-dependent. Drawing an analogy from enzymology, where protein kinase activity is regulated by specific cellular conditions and modifications, the solution is adapted. The research will systematically test NES function in vitro under defined, varying cellular conditions (redox, phosphorylation, co-factors) and correlate this with functional nuclear export assays synchronized to specific stages of viral replication. This will determine if these NESs act as regulated switches, active only during required phases of the viral life cycle, rather than as constitutive, independent export signals.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally resolved mapping of the HBc interactome during synchronized HBV trafficking
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs an inducible and compartment-restricted proximity labeling system to map the HBc interactome with high spatial and temporal resolution. A doxycycline-inducible promoter controls HBc-BioID2 expression to synchronize labeling with viral replication initiation. Chemogenetic tools, such as rapamycin-induced dimerization, are used to pulse-activate biotinylation specifically at the nuclear envelope, ER membranes, or cytoplasmic compartments at defined hours post-induction. By comparing the biotinylated proteomes from these discrete pulses, the system generates dynamic interaction maps that distinguish HBc's roles in genome transport, capsid assembly, and egress, overcoming the confounding aggregation of the original method.

ideas.title: Investigate viral NES mimicry as a novel mechanism of beta-cell dysfunction in polygenic T2D models
ideas.description: This study proposes that the pathological nuclear export mechanisms central to viral replication, like the Hepatitis B virus core protein's NES function, may have a metabolic disease parallel. We will test if proteins critical for beta-cell function and identity, which are dysregulated in polygenic rodent models of type 2 diabetes (e.g., NZO mouse), acquire improper nuclear export signals or partner with exportins, leading to their premature expulsion from the nucleus. By applying molecular virology tools (NES blocking peptides, CRM1 inhibition) to islets from these complex T2D models, we aim to determine if blocking this aberrant export restores normal nuclear localization of key transcription factors (e.g., PDX1, MAFA, FOXO1) and improves beta-cell function, thereby revealing a new 'viral-mimetic' pathway in diabetes etiology.

ideas.title: Phosphoproteomic mapping of metabolic signaling cascades to test inflammation as a secondary consequence
ideas.description: This fused project applies high-throughput phosphoproteomics to cells treated with vanillic acid (VA) with a specific analytical focus on deconvoluting the temporal hierarchy of signaling events. The core design compares the phosphoproteomic signature of VA under normal conditions versus conditions where key metabolic regulators (e.g., AMPK) are genetically or pharmacologically inhibited. By doing so, it directly tests whether VA-induced phosphorylation changes in inflammatory pathways (e.g., NF-κB, MAPK) are abolished when primary metabolic signaling is blocked, providing mechanistic evidence for or against inflammation reduction as a secondary consequence. This causal, network-based approach moves beyond correlation to establish a testable signaling hierarchy for VA's pleiotropic effects.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Guided Prediction and Validation of Phenolic Acid Synergy for Enhanced Glucose Uptake
ideas.description: This study first employs a computational and literature-driven approach to rationally select phenolic acid pairs predicted to act on distinct, complementary nodes in the cellular glucose uptake pathway (e.g., upstream signaling vs. transporter activity). Selected pairs are then rigorously tested in a differentiated muscle or adipose cell model, measuring not only glucose uptake but also key pathway activation markers to confirm the proposed dual-target mechanism. This strategy replaces blind combination screening with a hypothesis-driven framework, ensuring results are interpretable, mechanistically grounded, and provide a stronger foundation for subsequent in vivo research.

ideas.title: Use phosphoproteomics to map host kinase signaling networks targeted by HBV core protein nuclear export
ideas.description: This study applies high-throughput phosphoproteomics to cells infected with wild-type HBV and HBV mutants where the HBc nuclear export signal (NES) in ARD-II/IV is blocked. By comparing the phosphorylation landscapes, we will identify host kinase/phosphatase signaling networks specifically activated or suppressed by the NES function required for viral DNA synthesis. This map will reveal novel host dependencies of the HBV lifecycle. Subsequently, we will test if pharmacologically modulating these identified pathways with signaling inhibitors, including natural compounds like vanillic acid, can phenocopy the replication defect of NES mutants, offering a host-targeted antiviral strategy.

ideas.title: Quantitative affinity threshold defines ARD-derived weak NLS function
ideas.description: This redesigned idea first establishes a biophysical benchmark for what constitutes a functional weak NLS by quantitatively measuring the binding affinity of purified ARD-I and ARD-III fragments to importin-α in a cell-free system. These in vitro results are then functionally tested in a cellular competition assay, where the ARD fragment must restore nuclear localization of a reporter cargo against a titrated inhibitor of endogenous importin-α/NLS interactions. This two-tiered approach definitively separates specific, importin-mediated nuclear import from non-specific accumulation, testing whether any ARD fragment meets both the biophysical and functional criteria to operate as a genuine, autonomous NLS.

ideas.title: Testing ARD-I/III as weak NLS via a transcriptional amplification and localization assay
ideas.description: This idea challenges the absolute co-dependency of ARD-I/III on a strong NLS by testing if they possess latent, weak nuclear localization activity that can function under non-physiological conditions. Drawing an analogy from enzyme engineering, where weak catalysts are tested under high substrate concentration and amplification, we adapt this solution to nuclear import. The adapted research idea is to employ a highly sensitive, quantifiable nuclear localization reporter (e.g., split-GFP) and test ARD-I/III constructs under massive overexpression and with specific phospho-mimetic or other modifications. This approach will determine if these domains can ever function as independent, albeit weak, NLS signals when removed from their native regulatory context and pushed to extremes, thereby probing the fundamental modularity and hierarchy of nuclear targeting signals.

ideas.title: Targeted Hepatic Delivery of a TAP/NXF1-HBc ARD Inhibitor to Block HBV Capsid Nuclear Export
ideas.description: This project aims to develop a novel anti-HBV therapeutic by fusing two concepts: the molecular virology of the essential HBc-TAP/NXF1 interaction for viral nucleocapsid nuclear export, and advanced drug delivery strategies. The core objective is to design and package a peptide or small molecule inhibitor of this specific protein-protein interaction into a non-oral, liver-targeted delivery vehicle, such as a lipid nanoparticle or a galactosylated conjugate. This approach bypasses the poor bioavailability and systemic exposure of oral administration, delivering the inhibitor directly to hepatocytes to precisely block capsid egress and viral assembly. The synergy lies in creating a highly specific, tissue-localized antiviral that could circumvent resistance and toxicity issues associated with broad-acting, systemically delivered drugs.

ideas.title: Context-Dependent NES Activity as a Biomarker for VA Efficacy in a Human Prediabetes Trial
ideas.description: This study embeds a mechanistic virology investigation within a pilot clinical trial of Vanadyl Acetylacetonate (VA) for prediabetes. Blood samples from trial participants will be collected at multiple timepoints to isolate peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs). The functional dependence of ARD-II and IV NES on specific cellular conditions (e.g., metabolic state, inflammatory milieu) will be tested ex vivo in these patient-derived cells. This approach directly links the contextual activity of viral nuclear export signals to the dynamic human metabolic landscape, aiming to identify whether NES functionality serves as a novel, personalized biomarker predicting VA's efficacy on insulin resistance markers.

ideas.title: Model-specific optimization of VA therapy for genetically diverse T2D pathophysiology
ideas.description: This study will systematically determine the dose-response and time-course efficacy of vanadium (VA) compounds across multiple, genetically distinct rodent models of type 2 diabetes (T2D), including polygenic, monogenic, and chemically-induced models, alongside the traditional high-fat diet model. By applying identical, rigorous pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic optimization protocols to each model, it will identify whether a single VA regimen is broadly effective or if model-specific optima exist. This approach directly tests the hypothesis that therapeutic efficacy is contingent on the specific metabolic pathways disrupted in different diabetic etiologies. The results will provide a crucial preclinical framework for potentially stratifying human T2D patients for VA-based therapies based on disease subtype.

ideas.title: Synthetic reconstitution defines the minimal sufficient and necessary context for ARD-II/IV NES function
ideas.description: This project moves beyond observational testing to establish a causal, mechanistic understanding of NES context-dependency. It employs a synthetic biology approach to construct minimal, defined cellular environments—such as engineered membraneless compartments or staged replication organelles—into which modular NES-cargo reporters and specific components of the nuclear export machinery can be titrated. By systematically reconstituting function from the bottom-up and performing loss/gain-of-context experiments in living cells, the research will identify the precise biochemical and biophysical conditions (e.g., a threshold RanGTP gradient, absence of specific inhibitors, or presence of co-factors) that are both sufficient and necessary for ARD-II and IV NES activity. This shifts the question from 'if' these NESs are context-dependent to 'what specific contextual elements constitute the functional requirement'.

ideas.title: Map the host cell protein interactome perturbed by phenolic acid combinations during HBV capsid trafficking
ideas.description: This study applies proximity labeling (BioID/APEX) to map the interactome of the Hepatitis B virus core protein (HBc) during intracellular trafficking, but under the novel condition of treatment with synergistic combinations of phenolic acids like vanillic acid. The goal is to identify how these bioactive compound combinations functionally rewire the host-pathogen interface by altering transient interactions between HBc and host cell proteins involved in metabolism, trafficking, and innate immunity. This approach moves beyond cataloging interactions or measuring a single phenotypic output, instead directly revealing the proteomic network perturbations that constitute the mechanism of synergistic antiviral or host-directed activity. The resulting dataset would provide a unique map of druggable host pathways whose engagement by phenolic acid combinations disrupts the viral life cycle.

ideas.title: Determine how phosphorylation-regulated NES/NLS switching in the HBc ARD controls the spatiotemporal dynamics of viral genome synthesis and encapsidation
ideas.description: This fused project will investigate the hypothesis that phosphorylation of the HBc ARD acts as a dynamic switch, toggling the functional dominance between its NLS and NES motifs to spatially regulate the viral replication cycle. By creating phosphomimetic and phospho-deficient HBc mutants with specifically disabled NES functions, we will directly test how phosphorylation state dictates the nuclear retention or export of HBc and its bound viral pregenomic RNA/DNA, thereby controlling the transition from nuclear DNA synthesis to cytoplasmic capsid assembly. This approach synergistically combines functional blockade with mechanistic regulation to reveal how post-translational modification of the ARD temporally partitions replication steps, offering a new paradigm for understanding HBV persistence and a potential target for disrupting replication compartmentalization.

ideas.title: Computational prediction of small molecule disruptors of co-dependent NLS recognition by importins
ideas.description: This project integrates computational molecular docking and structural analysis to predict small molecules that can selectively bind to the interface of importin proteins (e.g., importin-α/β), specifically blocking their interaction with co-dependent NLS motifs such as those found in Hepatitis B core protein (HBc). Starting with vanillic acid as a scaffold, the approach will screen for derivatives that computationally dock into the NLS-binding groove of importins, prioritizing compounds predicted to disrupt the ARD-I/ARD-III composite signal. The synergy enables a shift from merely identifying novel protein targets for a compound to rationally designing compounds that act as precise inhibitors of a critical viral-host protein interaction, with potential applications in antiviral therapy.

ideas.title: Preclinical-to-Clinical Translation: A PK/PD-Guided Phase 0/I Study to Establish a Bioactive Dosing Regimen for Vanillic Acid in Humans Prior to Efficacy Testing
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first conducts a foundational human study to solve the critical translational problem of dosing. A small-scale, adaptive Phase 0/I trial will administer vanillic acid (VA) to both healthy and prediabetic volunteers using multiple routes (e.g., oral, formulated oral) and schedules. Intensive PK profiling and PD assessments in accessible tissues (e.g., muscle via biopsy for p-ACC, SIRT1 targets) will define the regimen that reliably engages the intended molecular pathway. Only this human-validated, bioactive regimen will then be advanced into a subsequent pilot efficacy trial, de-risking the major biological uncertainty and ensuring the efficacy trial tests a physiologically relevant intervention.

ideas.title: Causal disentanglement of metabolic and inflammatory pathways using orthogonal perturbation and temporal multi-omics
ideas.description: This project directly tests causality by applying specific metabolic interventions that lack known anti-inflammatory mechanisms and specific anti-inflammatory interventions that lack known metabolic effects. Using a longitudinal study in a metabolically compromised model, it employs frequent serial sampling for metabolomic, lipidomic, and inflammatory cytokine profiling to map the precise order of changes. Crucially, it combines these perturbations—forcing a metabolic improvement while pharmacologically blocking key inflammatory signaling nodes, and forcing inflammation reduction while inhibiting metabolic improvement pathways—to determine if one pathway is obligate for the other.

ideas.title: Adapting Nuclear Export Blockade Strategies from HIV-1 Rev to HBV HBc
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from HIV-1 Rev protein research to the HBV HBc protein problem. In HIV-1, blocking the Rev protein's nuclear export signal (NES) with inhibitors or dominant-negative mutants successfully sequestered viral RNA in the nucleus, halting replication. We propose to adapt this by creating specific dominant-negative HBc mutants with point mutations in its ARD-II and IV NES motifs. By co-expressing these mutants with wild-type HBV, they will act as competitive inhibitors, binding nuclear export machinery or viral components in the nucleus. This 'molecular sponge' strategy will directly test the necessity of HBc nuclear export for the cytoplasmic phase of viral DNA synthesis and downstream lifecycle events, providing a precise tool to dissect this function.

ideas.title: Investigate if VA-induced metabolic disruption in lean subjects alters host kinase activity to dysregulate HBc phospho-switching and subcellular trafficking
ideas.description: This study tests the hypothesis that vitamin A (VA) supplementation in lean, healthy subjects induces a state of metabolic dysregulation that, in turn, alters host cellular kinase/phosphatase activity. The altered kinase landscape is then investigated for its specific impact on the phosphorylation-dependent molecular switch of the Hepatitis B virus core protein (HBc) ARD domain. By doing so, it directly links a nutritional/metabolic intervention to the precise regulation of a viral life cycle determinant (NLS/NES activity and nucleocytoplasmic shuttling), exploring whether a 'healthy' metabolic state protects against viral manipulation of host signaling pathways.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Precursor State' Model from Immunology to Metabolic Interventions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that VA has a uniform effect by drawing an analogy from immunology, where interventions are categorized and tested based on the pre-existing state of the system (healthy/naïve vs. diseased/activated). The analogous solution is to design studies that fundamentally distinguish between prophylactic and therapeutic modes of action. Adapted to metabolic research, this means structuring a VA trial not just with healthy and diseased cohorts, but with distinct hypotheses for each: testing if VA acts as a 'metabolic primer' that enhances resilience and function in healthy, non-insulin-resistant subjects, versus a 'metabolic corrective' that repairs specific dysregulations only apparent in insulin-resistant states. The study would measure different primary endpoints (e.g., optimization markers vs. correction markers) to determine if the benefit and mechanism of action genuinely depend on the pre-existing metabolic state.

ideas.title: Adapting a Phase 0 Microdosing Trial Design from Oncology to Scale VA Testing in Prediabetes
ideas.description: To efficiently scale testing of Vanillic Acid (VA) for insulin resistance, we adapt the Phase 0 microdosing trial concept from oncology. In that field, sub-therapeutic doses are used in small human cohorts to rapidly gather preliminary data on drug-target engagement. We will conduct a microdose trial in prediabetic humans, using advanced metabolomic and optional tissue transcriptomic profiling to confirm VA's predicted mechanism (e.g., PPAR modulation) in human systems. This approach de-risks and informs the design of the subsequent, larger pilot efficacy trial by providing early human proof of mechanism.

ideas.title: Inflammation as a Symptom: Testing the Metabolic 'Root Cause' Hypothesis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that reducing inflammation is a primary therapeutic mechanism for metabolic diseases. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where treating a symptom (e.g., algal bloom) is less effective than treating its root cause (nutrient runoff), we adapt this solution to biomedical research. The adapted research idea is to test if anti-inflammatory effects are a consequence of improved metabolism by using interventions (e.g., specific modulators of lipid oxidation or mitochondrial biogenesis) designed to improve metabolic function without direct anti-inflammatory properties. The core experiment would then rigorously measure the sequence and dependency of inflammatory marker reduction following the metabolic improvement, thereby determining if inflammation is secondary to the metabolic 'root cause'.

ideas.title: Temporal resolution of VA-induced metabolic reprogramming: linking live-cell flux dynamics to single-cell metabolomic states
ideas.description: This study overcomes the static limitation of snapshot metabolomics by creating a temporal map of vitamin A's effects on hepatocyte metabolism. A microfluidic live-cell imaging platform tracks real-time changes in key metabolic cofactors and metabolites using fluorescent biosensors in individual primary hepatocytes over hours to days post-VA exposure. These longitudinal flux profiles are then anchored to deep, single-cell metabolomic states obtained via mass spectrometry from the same cells at critical timepoints. This integrated approach distinguishes transient adaptations from sustained reprogramming, reveals metabolic trajectory heterogeneity, and identifies the sequence of metabolic events initiated by VA signaling in individual liver cells.

ideas.title: Apply compartment-specific proximity labeling to map the HBc interactome during intracellular trafficking
ideas.description: This idea adapts a compartment-specific proximity labeling solution from neuroscience to virology. In synaptic biology, APEX2 fused to synaptic proteins, combined with spatially or temporally restricted biotin-phenol delivery, has been used to map the highly dynamic protein interactomes at specific sub-neuronal compartments. We adapt this by applying compartment-specific APEX2 labeling to the Hepatitis B core (HBc) protein. By engineering HBc-APEX2 fusions and using strategies like organelle-restricted reagent activation or synchronization with trafficking stages, we can perform proximity labeling selectively in the ER, Golgi, endosomal system, or nucleus during HBc trafficking. This will map the transient and proximal interacting partners of HBc with spatial and temporal resolution, revealing distinct interaction networks critical for capsid assembly, trafficking, and viral replication.

ideas.title: Systematic Screening of Vanillic Acid Co-Administration Regimens Using a Drug Combination Index Framework
ideas.description: This idea proposes to investigate the synergistic effects of vanillic acid with other phenolic acids for enhanced glucose uptake by drawing an analogy from antimicrobial chemotherapy. In that field, the Chou-Talalay Combination Index (CI) method is used to quantitatively define synergistic, additive, or antagonistic interactions between antibiotics through systematic dose-response matrices. Adapted to this context, the research would involve performing in vitro glucose uptake assays in relevant cell models (e.g., L6 myotubes or 3T3-L1 adipocytes) with vanillic acid and a partner phenolic acid (e.g., ferulic acid) across a range of concentrations, both alone and in combination. The resulting data would be analyzed using the CI model to calculate interaction indices, thereby moving beyond qualitative claims of 'synergy' to identify the precise molar ratios of phenolic acid pairs that yield genuinely synergistic enhancement of glucose uptake. This approach provides a rigorous, quantitative framework for optimizing combinatorial nutraceutical formulations.

ideas.title: Inverse: Does VA dysregulate adipocyte homeostasis to create a pre-pathological state in lean subjects?
ideas.description: This study tests the specific hypothesis that vitamin A (retinol/retinoic acid) supplementation in lean subjects induces a dysregulated, pre-pathological state in white adipose tissue by disrupting core adipocyte functions. Using a lean mouse model, we conduct a detailed time-course analysis post-VA supplementation, employing transcriptomics, lipidomics, and targeted metabolomics on adipose tissue to map early perturbations in lipolytic flux, de novo lipogenesis, and adipokine secretion. The design explicitly distinguishes between adaptive metabolic fluctuations and maladaptive dysregulation by correlating molecular changes with in vivo functional assays of adipose tissue health, thereby providing a mechanistic basis for any observed metabolic worsening.

ideas.title: Dual: Study VA in concurrent HFD and toxin model using a multi-target, synergistic defense strategy
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from corrosion engineering, where multi-functional coatings protect against combined environmental insults. The analogous problem of protecting a metal from simultaneous high-temperature oxidation and chemical pitting is solved using synergistic, multi-layered coatings with specific elements for each threat. Adapted to the original idea of investigating VA in a combined high-fat diet (HFD) and toxin model, the study will test a VA-based combination therapy. The core hypothesis is that pairing VA with a second agent targeting a complementary pathway (e.g., inflammation or oxidative stress from the toxin) will create a synergistic 'defense system,' offering superior protection against the dual-hit model of insulin resistance than VA alone.

ideas.title: Investigate VA's systemic insulin-sensitizing effects via a multi-tissue signaling network model
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecosystem ecology, where stable isotope tracing is used to map nutrient flows through an entire network of compartments. The analogous problem of understanding a compound's effect on a complex system is solved by tracking the labeled element through multiple interconnected nodes. Adapted to the research on vanillic acid (VA), the solution is to use isotopic glucose tracers in vivo alongside VA treatment, then measure tracer flux and insulin pathway activation (e.g., p-AKT, GLUT4 translocation) across a panel of insulin-sensitive tissues (muscle, adipose, liver, etc.). The data would be integrated into a multi-tissue signaling network model to determine if VA exerts its effects through a primary 'node' (e.g., adipose tissue inflammation reduction) that subsequently improves insulin signaling in peripheral tissues like muscle, thereby defining its holistic mechanism of action.

ideas.title: Simultaneous live imaging of GLUT2 trafficking and cytosolic glucose flux to dissect VA's direct action on hepatocyte glucose uptake
ideas.description: This redesigned study employs hepatocytes co-expressing a fluorescently tagged GLUT2 and a genetically encoded FRET-based glucose biosensor to enable concurrent, real-time visualization of transporter translocation and the resulting changes in intracellular glucose concentration. The system directly tests whether VA-induced GLUT2 movement is functionally coupled to increased glucose import. To isolate direct effects, acute perturbations of candidate signaling pathways are applied during imaging, allowing the differentiation of primary VA targets from secondary metabolic adaptations. This approach moves beyond descriptive trafficking kinetics to a causal, mechanistic analysis of VA's glucoregulatory action.

ideas.title: Transdermal and Sublingual Delivery of Vitamin A: Borrowing from Hormone Replacement Therapy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that oral administration is the optimal delivery route for Vitamin A (VA) by drawing an analogy from endocrinology. In hormone replacement therapy, oral delivery of steroids like estrogen is problematic due to extensive first-pass liver metabolism, which lowers bioavailability and increases side effects. The analogous solution was the development of transdermal and sublingual delivery systems, which allow direct entry into systemic circulation. Adapting this solution, the research would explore formulating VA (e.g., retinol or retinyl esters) into transdermal patches or sublingual tablets. This could significantly improve VA bioavailability by bypassing pre-systemic elimination, enable better controlled and sustained release, reduce the hepatotoxicity associated with high oral doses, and open possibilities for tissue-targeting through controlled systemic delivery, moving beyond the limitations of the oral-gut-liver pathway.

ideas.title: Stratified and adaptive precision dosing for VA therapy
ideas.description: This research program first identifies and validates key biomarkers (e.g., genetic variants in retinoid metabolism pathways, baseline serum retinol/RBP4) that predict differential response to VA. Using these biomarkers, the target population is stratified into distinct subgroups. Within each subgroup, an adaptive platform trial is conducted, employing Bayesian optimization to efficiently explore the dose-duration parameter space and converge on a subgroup-specific optimal regimen. This approach moves beyond a one-size-fits-all recommendation to a precision dosing strategy.

ideas.title: Apply network perturbation analysis from traffic engineering to VA-induced metabolic flux changes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from urban traffic engineering, where tracer vehicles are used to map system-wide flow redistribution after a network perturbation, to the problem of understanding valproic acid's effects on hepatocyte metabolism. In the analogous field, engineers inject GPS-equipped vehicles into traffic to quantify how a closure reroutes flow and creates bottlenecks. Adapted to the research context, we treat VA treatment as the perturbation and use a multi-tracer isotopic strategy (e.g., parallel 13C-glucose and 13C-glutamine) as the metabolic 'tracer vehicles.' By applying metabolic flux analysis and network modeling to the resulting enrichment data, we can move beyond static snapshots to dynamically map the detailed rerouting of hepatic glucose and lipid flux, identifying compensatory pathways and critical, vulnerable nodes in the metabolic network that are disrupted by the drug.
